meta.author: Leana Le
meta.purpose: Portfolio case studies with prototype-aware content
meta.notes: All projects are student projects. No fabricated metrics. Prototypes appear after their referenced sections.

case_studies.title: ProLog — Mobile Apprenticeship Companion
case_studies.subtitle: A journey companion for neurodivergent apprentices
case_studies.summary: A centralized apprenticeship companion helping neurodivergent apprentices track progress, reduce cognitive overload, and build confidence through clear visualization and structured tools.
case_studies.context: School project in collaboration with ConnectHER at BCIT

hook.content: Apprenticeship can take over 6,000 hours. That's years of work spread across job sites, classrooms, and bureaucratic systems — often without a clear sense of whether you're on track.

media_demo.caption: ProLog centralizes apprenticeship progress into one calm dashboard.

problem_framing.content:
Apprentices in British Columbia navigate a surprisingly fragmented system. Hours are logged in one place, competencies tracked in another, and certification requirements buried in dense PDFs.

For neurotypical users, this is frustrating. For neurodivergent apprentices—who may already struggle with executive function, time blindness, or information overload—it becomes a genuine barrier to completion.

The system wasn't designed to be hostile. It just wasn't designed with these users in mind.

methods: Persona development based on lived experience
Team reflection sessions (our team included neurodivergent members)
key_findings: Progress tracking is scattered across multiple platforms with no single source of truth
Official systems are desktop-first and difficult to use on job sites
Discrepancies between employer-logged and self-logged hours create anxiety
Neurodivergent users experience amplified cognitive load from inconsistent interfaces

do.title: Do
do.rule: Map fragmented systems into one clear progress model users can scan in seconds.
do.illustrationText: Single source of truth for hours and competencies

dont.title: Don't
dont.rule: Don't mirror the original bureaucracy inside the product interface.

media_demo.caption: Research translated into a dashboard-first information hierarchy.

lofi_phase.content:
Our early sketches were deliberately rough. We weren't trying to make things look good—we were trying to understand what information mattered most and how quickly someone could find it.

The dashboard concept emerged early. We kept asking ourselves: if an apprentice opens this app on a break, what should they see in the first three seconds?

Progress bars, hour counts, and next-step prompts became the core. Everything else was secondary.

hifi_phase.content: The hi-fi designs led with calm. Soft colours, generous whitespace, and typography that doesn't feel clinical. Every screen answers one question without making the user ask it. The dashboard became the emotional anchor — not just a data view, but a sense of progress.

images.caption: The dashboard provides immediate progress visibility

prototype.description: Explore the dashboard experience. Pay attention to how progress is communicated without requiring any taps.

feature_breakdown.intro: Each feature was designed to reduce one specific point of friction. Click through the prototype below to see how they work together.

features.title: Progress Dashboard
features.description: A centralized view of hours completed, competencies achieved, and certification progress. The answer to "where am I?" is always one tap away.
features.why: Apprentices told us they felt lost. The dashboard acts as a GPS — constant orientation, no digging.

features.title: Hours Discrepancy Detector
features.description: Compares user-logged hours against official records and flags mismatches. Users can document and report discrepancies directly.
features.why: Hour disputes are stressful and common. We gave users tools to advocate for themselves.

features.title: Financial Resources Hub
features.description: Links to grants, funding programs, and cost breakdowns for apprenticeship expenses — all in one place.
features.why: Financial stress came up in every research session. We could not solve it, but we could make it easier to navigate.

features.title: AI Practice Quizzes
features.description: Generates practice questions based on trade competencies to build confidence before official exams.
features.why: Many apprentices feel underprepared for certification. Low-stakes practice reduces that anxiety.

prototype.description: Walk through each feature. Start with the dashboard, then explore the hours tracker and quiz flow.

do.title: Do
do.rule: Tie every feature to one concrete user anxiety and make the payoff explicit.

dont.title: Don't
dont.rule: Don't ship utility features that lack a direct apprenticeship outcome.

media_demo.caption: Feature set prioritizes orientation, discrepancy confidence, and exam readiness.

validation.content: Users appreciated not having to hunt for information — it was just there. The hours tracker resonated most, addressing a previously unmet need in the most anxiety-inducing part of the process.
outcomes: Users reported feeling relieved by progress visibility
Hours tracker addressed a previously unmet need

media_demo.caption: Validation centered on whether users felt clearer and less anxious about progress.

what_i_learned.reflection: When users said they felt 'relieved,' they were responding to design decisions—not just information architecture.

what_i_learned.insight: Scope control is a design skill
what_i_learned.reflection: Saying no to good ideas was harder than coming up with them.
Seeing how far you've come matters as much as knowing what's next.
In student work, we don't always have access to large sample sizes. That doesn't make insights less real.

hero.marqueeText: CÅSE STUDIËS • PROLOG • MOBILE APPRENTICESHIP COMPANION

checklist.title: Case Study Checklist
checklist.subtitle: A quick anatomy check for the ProLog narrative.

items.title: Problem framing is user-specific
items.description: The write-up identifies why fragmented apprenticeship systems are especially harmful for neurodivergent users.

items.title: Role and team scope are explicit
items.description: The hero metadata clarifies technical and research ownership in an 8-person team context.

items.title: Process moves from discovery to design
items.description: Research, lo-fi, and hi-fi decisions are sequenced so readers can follow causality.

items.title: Outcome language stays evidence-led
items.description: Validation outcomes report observed user response without inflated claims.

items.title: Visuals support skim reading
items.description: Key screens include captions that communicate purpose even without full paragraph reading.

case_studies.title: InkLink — Collaborative Writing App
case_studies.subtitle: Breaking writer's block through collaboration
case_studies.summary: A chain-writing platform that reduces writer's block by turning writing into a shared, low-pressure activity where users contribute one passage at a time.
responsibilities: Led user research and concept validation

hero.marqueeText: CÅSE STUDIËS • INKLINK • COLLABORATIVE WRITING APP

hook.content:
Everyone has a story they never finished.

Ideas were there. Momentum wasn't.

InkLink started with one question: what if writing felt shared instead of solitary?

media_demo.caption: InkLink reframes writing as a shared, low-pressure chain.

problem_framing.content:
Writer's block isn't usually about running out of ideas. It's about pressure.

The blank page. The expectation of perfection. The lonely weight of being solely responsible for what comes next.

We wanted to design an experience that removed those barriers—not by making writing easier, but by making it shared.

methods: Peer surveys with amateur and hobbyist writers
Concept validation sessions to gauge interest
key_findings: Most creative writing happens on desktop or tablet—not mobile phones
Dense, text-heavy layouts discourage reading and contribution
New concepts require gentle onboarding; users won't explore without guidance

do.title: Do
do.rule: Prioritize scan-friendly structure and clear contribution prompts.

dont.title: Don't
dont.rule: Avoid dense copy blocks that raise cognitive load before writing starts.

media_demo.caption: Early concept for reducing entry friction with a single clear action.

lofi_phase.content: Our lo-fi work focused on one core interaction: contributing to a story. We sketched different approaches to showing story history, handling turn-taking, and branching narratives. The visuals were intentionally basic — we needed to test logic, not aesthetics.

feature_breakdown.intro: InkLink has three core modes of engagement. Explore them in the prototype below.

features.title: Public Story Chains
features.description: Open stories that anyone can contribute to. Writers join, add a passage, and pass the story forward.
features.why: Low barrier to entry. No commitment required—just jump in and write.

features.title: Private Writing Circles
features.description: Invite-only spaces for friends or writing groups. Turn order is set by the creator.
features.why: Some writers want collaboration without strangers. Private circles offer that control.

features.title: Timed Contributions
features.description: Optional time limits encourage momentum. If a turn expires, the next writer can step in.
features.why: Deadlines reduce overthinking. A 24-hour window is long enough to be fair, short enough to keep stories moving.

prototype.description: Start a new story, invite a friend, or join an existing chain. Follow the prompts to see each flow.

do.title: Do
do.rule: Design each mode with one obvious next step so users can start writing immediately.

dont.title: Don't
dont.rule: Don't overload the first screen with options, filters, and setup complexity.

media_demo.caption: The editor keeps participation lightweight so writers stay in flow.

validation.content: Testers described InkLink as 'fun.' The reduced pressure was noticeable. Users felt less precious about their contributions because they knew someone else would build on them. The time limits helped keep the experience moving.
outcomes: Reduced pressure led to less perfectionism
Time limits helped rather than hurt the experience

what_i_learned.reflection: Limiting options (one clear CTA, timed turns) reduced friction instead of adding it.
The newspaper aesthetic was fun but counterproductive.
We cut more than we added—and the product got better.

checklist.title: Case Study Checklist
checklist.subtitle: A quick anatomy check for this project write-up.

items.title: Project context and challenge are explicit
items.description: The case study opens with the core tension: pressure and perfectionism block participation.

items.title: Role and team are immediately visible
items.description: The hero metadata clarifies contribution boundaries before the reader enters process details.

items.title: Process is chronological and scannable
items.description: Research, exploration, and feature decisions are broken into short sections with clear labels.

items.title: Outcome evidence is present
items.description: Validation outcomes capture user response and why the design direction was retained.

items.title: Visuals include captions
items.description: Images carry descriptive captions so skimmers still get the narrative arc.

case_studies.title: SideQuest — Micro-Adventure Web App
case_studies.subtitle: Turning boredom into micro-adventures
case_studies.summary: A web app that helps users discover small, low-commitment activities to break routine, reframing free time as an opportunity rather than a void to fill.

hook.content: You have a free afternoon. Nothing planned. So you open your phone, scroll for thirty minutes, and end up back on the couch — paralyzed by options, defaulting to nothing.

media_demo.caption: SideQuest reframes idle time as small, low-pressure adventures.

problem_framing.content:
Boredom isn't really about having nothing to do. It's about having too much to choose from and not enough motivation to pick.

Every app promises endless possibilities. But endless possibilities require endless decisions. And decisions are exhausting.

We wanted to flip that model. Instead of showing users everything they could do, we'd suggest one thing they might enjoy—and make it easy to say yes.

methods: Persona creation based on peer interviews
Competitive analysis of activity discovery apps
key_findings: Users want low-pressure activities that don't require planning
Choice overload causes hesitation and often leads to inaction

do.title: Do
do.rule: Reduce choice overload with a guided default that gets users moving quickly.

dont.title: Don't
dont.rule: Don't force users through heavy setup before they see their first useful suggestion.

media_demo.caption: Research findings drove a quick-start interface with minimal decisions.

lofi_phase.content:
Our early wireframes had too many buttons.

Filters for location, duration, cost, category—all logical, all counterproductive. Users would hover over options without committing to any of them.

We stripped it back. One primary action: 'Give me a quest.' Everything else became optional.

images.caption: Initial designs with too many options

hifi_phase.content: The hi-fi designs leaned into warmth and play. A mascot guides without hand-holding. Colour pops where it should — on the quest card, not the chrome. Language is casual: "quest" not "activity", "try it" not "add to list". The goal was to feel more like a friend nudging you out the door than an app telling you what to do.

images.caption: The mascot welcomes users with a single clear action
Quest suggestions are simple and actionable
Just enough information to get started

prototype.description: Tap 'Give me a quest' and see what happens. Try refreshing for a different suggestion.

feature_breakdown.intro: SideQuest has three core elements. Explore each in the prototype below.

features.title: Quest Suggestions
features.description: Low-commitment activities framed as optional adventures — each with a title, brief description, and estimated time.
features.why: The word "quest" makes mundane activities feel interesting. Framing matters more than content.

features.title: Friendly Guide
features.description: A small mascot that appears throughout the experience, offering encouragement without being intrusive.
features.why: The mascot adds warmth. It makes the app feel like a companion, not a tool.

features.title: Quick Start Flow
features.description: A quest in two taps. No account required, no preferences to configure.
features.why: Every step between "I am bored" and "here is something to do" is a chance to lose the user. We cut every one we could.

prototype.description: Start from the home screen. Get a quest, save it for later, or ask for something different.

do.title: Do
do.rule: Keep language playful and actionable so users commit before overthinking.

dont.title: Don't
dont.rule: Don't over-index on filters and options that delay momentum.

media_demo.caption: Core features optimize for immediacy: pick a quest, start, and continue.

validation.content: Users responded to the playfulness. The word 'quest' made people smile. The mascot got positive comments. The low-pressure framing worked—users felt more willing to try things because the stakes felt low.

media_demo.caption: Validation measured whether the low-pressure framing increased willingness to act.

what_i_learned.reflection: Getting users to act once is more valuable than giving them ten options.
Every element either builds confidence or creates doubt. There's no neutral.

what_i_learned.insight: Research, design, and code inform each other
what_i_learned.reflection: Being involved in all three made each one stronger.

hero.marqueeText: CÅSE STUDIËS • SIDEQUEST • MICRO-ADVENTURE WEB APP

checklist.title: Case Study Checklist
checklist.subtitle: A quick anatomy check for the SideQuest story arc.

items.title: The behavioral problem is clear
items.description: The case study defines boredom as decision fatigue, not lack of options.

items.title: Contribution scope is explicit
items.description: Role and team metadata establish end-to-end ownership across research, design, and build.

items.title: Design decisions tie to findings
items.description: The process shows why simplification and guided prompts replaced option-heavy flows.

items.title: Outcomes are concrete and honest
items.description: Validation captures engagement signals without overstating quantitative impact.

items.title: Visual storytelling is scannable
items.description: Screens and captions communicate feature intent even for quick readers.
